What it does: A class should have only one reason to change — one job only.
Assignment Violation: GymManager does 3 jobs:- Store member data, Print info, Save to file
Why it works: Each class has one clear job. If printing changes, only MemberPrinterRefactored changes.

What it does: Open for extension, closed for modification. Add new behavior without changing existing code.
Assignment Violation: GymScheduler uses if-else for each session type. Adding "Pilates" requires changing GymScheduler.
Assignment Solution: Use interface + implementations:WorkoutSessionRefactored ← YogaSessionRefactored, CardioSessionRefactored, etc.

What it does: Subclasses must be substitutable for their parent without breaking behavior.
Assignment Violation: LockedEquipment inherits release() but throws exception — breaks the contract.
Why it works: No more broken contracts. Only equipment that can be released implements the interface.

What it does: Don't force classes to implement irrelevant methods. Use small, role-specific interfaces.
Assignment Violation: GymUser has 3 methods, but RegularMember only needs bookClass().
Assignment Solution: Split into 3 small interfaces:BookableRefactored, ManageableRefactored, ReportViewableRefactoredRegularMemberRefactored ← only BookableRefactored
Why it works: RegularMemberRefactored implements 1 method only, not 3 irrelevant ones.

What it does: High-level modules depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations. Inject dependencies.
Assignment Violation: TrainingCoach creates WeightTrainingPlan directly inside — hard dependency.
Assignment Solution: TrainingCoachRefactored takes TrainingPlanRefactored interface via constructor.
Why it works: Can inject WeightTrainingPlanRefactored OR CardioTrainingPlanRefactored — same coach class works with both.
